title: How Much Does House Cleaning Cost? [2025 Calculator]
metaDescription: Get a free house cleaning cost estimate based on your home size and cleaning type. No email required, no national averages. See real rates for your home.
h1: How Much Does House Cleaning Cost? Get a Free Estimate Based on Your Home

sections.heading: What You'll Pay for a Clean Home
sections.body:
Let's cut to the chase. You want to know how much house cleaning costs — and you don't want to hand over your email to find out. I get it. I've been there. Most online estimators ask for your contact info before showing you a number, and then they give you some national average that doesn't match your city or your home. That's not helpful.

So here's what we built: a calculator that uses real pricing data from sources like HomeAdvisor, Angi, Thumbtack, and The Spruce. No email required. No sales calls. Just a clean estimate based on your home's square footage, the type of cleaning you need, and how often you want it done.

House cleaning costs range from $100 to $500 per visit depending on your home size and the service level. Standard cleaning for a 1,500-square-foot home runs about $175 on average. Deep cleaning? That's typically $300. Move-out cleaning can hit $350 or more. But your actual cost depends on where you live, how dirty your home is, and whether you want extras like oven cleaning or window washing.

Honestly, the best way to get a number that matters is to use the calculator above. But if you want to understand what goes into those numbers, keep reading.

sections.heading: House Cleaning Cost Calculator
Average House Cleaning Costs by Type and Home Size
sections.body: Here's a quick reference table based on real data from multiple sources. These are typical ranges — your actual cost may be higher or lower depending on your location and the specific cleaning company.

tableData.footnote: Ranges based on typical per-cleaning pricing. Hourly rates: standard $50-$80/hr, deep $75-$110/hr. Move-out is often priced per square foot at $0.15-$0.30/sq ft.

sections.heading: What Affects the Price of House Cleaning?
sections.body:
Not all cleaning services charge the same way. Some charge by the hour. Others charge a flat rate per visit. And some charge by the square foot. Here's what you need to know about each pricing model.

**Hourly rates** are common for standard cleaning. You'll pay between $50 and $80 per hour. A small home (under 1,000 sq ft) typically takes 1.5 to 2.5 hours. A medium home takes 2.5 to 4 hours. Large homes (2,000-3,000 sq ft) need 4 to 6 hours. Extra large homes? Plan on 5 to 8 hours.

**Flat-rate pricing** is more common for deep cleaning and move-out cleaning. Companies quote a single price for the whole job. This is nice because you know the cost upfront — no surprises if the job takes longer than expected.

**Per-square-foot pricing** is another option. Standard cleaning runs $0.10 to $0.18 per square foot. Deep cleaning is $0.18 to $0.30 per square foot. Move-out cleaning is $0.15 to $0.30 per square foot.

And then there are add-ons. Oven cleaning adds $30 to $60. Fridge cleaning adds $25 to $50. Interior window cleaning runs $5 to $15 per window. Carpet cleaning adds $25 to $75 per room. These extras can add up fast.

In my experience, the biggest factor is how often you schedule cleanings. Weekly service gets you a 15% to 30% discount. Biweekly gets 10% to 20% off. Monthly gets 5% to 10% off. So if you can commit to regular cleanings, you'll save money per visit.

sections.heading: Standard Cleaning vs. Deep Cleaning vs. Move-Out Cleaning: Which Do You Need?
sections.body: Choosing the right type of cleaning saves you money and gets you the results you actually want. Here's how they compare.

comparisonItems.name: Standard Cleaning
comparisonItems.description: Surface-level cleaning of all main rooms. Includes dusting, vacuuming, mopping, wiping counters, cleaning bathrooms, and making beds. Usually takes 2-3 hours for a medium home.
comparisonItems.bestFor: Maintaining a clean home on a regular schedule. Good for weekly, biweekly, or monthly service.

comparisonItems.name: Deep Cleaning
comparisonItems.description: Everything in standard cleaning plus detailed work: scrubbing baseboards, cleaning inside cabinets, washing walls, deep-cleaning appliances, and tackling grout. Takes 4-6 hours for a medium home.
comparisonItems.bestFor: Spring cleaning, moving into a new home, or when you haven't had a professional clean in a while.

comparisonItems.name: Move-Out Cleaning
comparisonItems.description: Similar to deep cleaning but with a focus on getting your security deposit back. Includes inside cabinets, appliances, windows, and often carpet cleaning. Usually priced per square foot.
comparisonItems.bestFor: Renters moving out, homeowners selling their home, or landlords preparing a property.

sections.heading: How to Get the Best Deal on House Cleaning
sections.body:
You don't have to pay top dollar. Here are a few strategies that actually work.

**Book recurring service.** If you schedule weekly cleanings, you can save 15% to 30% per visit. Biweekly saves 10% to 20%. Monthly saves 5% to 10%. That's real money over a year.

**Bundle add-ons.** If you need oven cleaning and window washing, ask if they offer a package discount. Many companies will knock off $10-$20 if you book multiple extras.

**Get multiple quotes.** Use the calculator above to get a baseline, then call 2-3 local companies. Ask for a quote over the phone — don't let them schedule an in-person visit just to give you a price.

**Consider an independent cleaner.** National chains charge more because they have overhead. Local independent cleaners often charge $10-$20 less per hour and do just as good a job. Check their reviews on Thumbtack or Nextdoor.

**Time it right.** Deep cleaning in the off-season (not spring) can sometimes get you a discount. Some companies offer lower rates in winter or summer when demand is lower.

Based on real data, the sweet spot for most homeowners is biweekly standard cleaning at $150-$200 per visit. That keeps your home consistently clean without breaking the bank.

sections.heading: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body: Still have questions? Here are answers to the most common ones.

faqs.question: How much does it cost to clean a 3-bedroom house?
faqs.answer: A 3-bedroom house is typically in the medium category (1,000-2,000 sq ft). Standard cleaning runs $150 to $250 per visit. Deep cleaning is $250 to $400. Move-out cleaning is $300 to $450.

faqs.question: Do you tip house cleaners? How much?
faqs.answer: Tipping is not required but appreciated. $10-$20 per cleaner per visit is standard. For a deep clean or move-out, $20-$50 per cleaner is fair. Some people tip 15-20% of the total cost.

faqs.question: What's included in a standard house cleaning?
faqs.answer: Typically: dusting all surfaces, vacuuming carpets and rugs, mopping hard floors, cleaning bathrooms (toilets, sinks, showers, mirrors), wiping kitchen counters and sinks, and making beds. It does NOT include inside cabinets, appliances, windows, or walls.

faqs.question: How long does a house cleaning take?
faqs.answer: Small homes take 1.5 to 2.5 hours. Medium homes take 2.5 to 4 hours. Large homes take 4 to 6 hours. Extra large homes take 5 to 8 hours. Deep cleaning adds about 50% more time.

faqs.question: Is it cheaper to clean your own house?
faqs.answer: Yes, but you're trading time for money. Professional cleaners are 2-3x faster than most people. If your time is worth $50/hour or more, hiring a cleaner is actually more economical.

faqs.question: Do cleaning companies provide their own supplies?
faqs.answer: Most do. They bring their own cleaning products, tools, and equipment. If you prefer eco-friendly products or have specific allergies, ask ahead — most companies can accommodate.

faqs.question: How much does move-out cleaning cost?
faqs.answer: $250 to $500 per visit depending on home size. It's often priced at $0.15 to $0.30 per square foot. For a 1,500 sq ft home, expect $300 to $450.

faqs.question: What's the difference between standard and deep cleaning?
faqs.answer: Deep cleaning includes everything standard does plus scrubbing baseboards, cleaning inside cabinets and appliances, washing walls, and deep-cleaning grout and tile. It's more thorough and takes longer.

faqs.question: How much does house cleaning cost per hour?
faqs.answer: $50 to $80 per hour for standard cleaning. Deep cleaning costs $75 to $110 per hour.

faqs.question: How much does it cost to clean a 2,000 sq ft house?
faqs.answer: Standard cleaning: $150 to $250. Deep cleaning: $250 to $400. Move-out cleaning: $300 to $450.

faqs.question: Do I need to be home for a house cleaning?
faqs.answer: No, most cleaners work fine if you leave a key or give them a garage code. Just make sure pets are secured.

faqs.question: How often should I get my house cleaned?
faqs.answer: Every 2 weeks is the sweet spot for most families. Weekly if you have pets or kids. Monthly if you keep up with tidying.

sections.heading: What Affects House Cleaning Costs?
sections.body:
Several factors push the price up or down. House size is the biggest one. A small apartment under 1,000 sq ft might take just 2 hours. An extra-large home over 3,000 sq ft could need 6-7 hours. That's a big difference.

Cleaning type matters too. Standard cleaning is your basic dusting, vacuuming, mopping, and bathroom scrub. Deep cleaning goes way further—baseboards, inside cabinets, behind appliances. Move-out cleaning sits somewhere in between but often includes walls and windows.

Frequency discounts are real. Book weekly and you can save 15-30% off the per-cleaning price. Biweekly gets you 10-20% off. Even monthly saves 5-10%. The calculator doesn't apply these automatically because every company handles discounts differently, but keep them in mind when budgeting.

Location also plays a role. Prices in New York or San Francisco run higher than in rural areas. Our estimates use national averages, so adjust up or down based on where you live.

sections.heading: Standard vs. Deep vs. Move-Out: Which One Do You Need?
sections.body:
Not sure which service fits? Here's the breakdown. Standard cleaning is perfect for maintenance—weekly or biweekly visits to keep things tidy. Deep cleaning is what you hire when you haven't cleaned in months, or before a party. Move-out cleaning is specifically for rental turnover and usually includes things like oven, fridge, and window cleaning.

Honestly, if you're moving out, just go with move-out cleaning. It's typically cheaper than booking a deep clean plus all the add-ons separately. And landlords expect it.

For regular upkeep, standard cleaning at $100-$250 per visit is your best bet. Add deep cleaning once or twice a year to reset the grime.

sections.heading: Add-On Services: What Extras Will Cost You
sections.body:
Most cleaning companies offer optional extras. They're not always included in the base price. Here's what you can expect to pay:

- Oven Cleaning: $30–$60 each (typical $45)
- Fridge Cleaning: $25–$50 each (typical $35)
- Window Cleaning (interior): $5–$15 per window (typical $10)
- Carpet Cleaning: $25–$75 per room (typical $50)

These add up fast. If you have 10 windows, that's $50–$150 extra. The calculator totals them for you, so you'll see the full picture before you book.

One thing I've found: always ask if the base cleaning includes surface wiping of appliances. Some companies do. Some don't. Then you can decide if you really need the full oven clean or just a wipe-down.

Step 2: Choose the cleaning type. Standard, Deep, or Move-Out.

Step 3: Check any add-ons you need. Oven, fridge, windows, carpet—select all that apply.

Step 4: Read your estimate. You'll see low, typical, and high ranges instantly.

faqs.answer: Standard cleaning focuses on surfaces—dusting, vacuuming, mopping, and basic bathroom scrub. Deep cleaning includes all that plus baseboards, window tracks, inside cabinets, and behind appliances. Deep cleaning takes 2–4 hours longer.

h1: Cost of Deep House Cleaning: Calculator & Pricing Guide

sections.heading: How Much Does Deep House Cleaning Cost?
sections.body:
Deep cleaning your house costs more than a standard clean. A lot more. Based on data from HomeAdvisor, Angi, and Thumbtack, you're looking at $200 to $400 per cleaning for a typical home. That's nearly double the $100 to $250 you'd pay for standard maintenance cleaning.

But here's the thing — most online calculators don't have a "deep clean" checkbox. They treat every cleaning the same. That's wrong. And it's why you're here.

I've been there. When I moved out of my last apartment, I assumed a standard clean would cut it. Landlord disagreed. Cost me an extra $150 in last-minute deep cleaning. Don't make that mistake.

This calculator is different. It separates deep cleaning from standard cleaning. It accounts for your home's size, how often you clean, and optional add-ons like oven or fridge cleaning. No email required. Just slide the inputs and see your estimate instantly.

sections.heading: Standard vs Deep Cleaning: What's the Difference?
sections.body: Here's a quick breakdown of pricing based on real data. Notice the hourly rate jumps from $50-$80 for standard to $75-$110 for deep cleaning. That's because deep cleaning takes longer and requires more elbow grease.

sections.heading: Why Deep Cleaning Costs More — and Where Your Money Goes
sections.body:
Deep cleaning isn't just a longer version of standard cleaning. It's a different service entirely.

Standard cleaning covers surface-level tasks: dusting, vacuuming, wiping counters, mopping floors. It's maintenance. You do it every week or two.

Deep cleaning gets into the cracks. Baseboards, window tracks, behind appliances, inside cabinets, light fixtures, ceiling fans. Things that build up grime over months or years. Honestly, it's the difference between a quick wipe-down and a full scrub.

For a medium house (1,000-2,000 sq ft), a deep clean takes 2.5 to 4 hours. At $75-$110 per hour, that's $187.50 to $440 before add-ons. The calculator above uses these exact ranges.

Add-ons can push the total higher. Oven cleaning runs $30-$60. Fridge cleaning is $25-$50. Interior windows cost $5-$15 each. Carpet cleaning adds $25-$75 per room. The calculator lets you toggle these on and off to see how they affect your total.

sections.heading: How House Size Affects Deep Cleaning Costs
sections.body:
Size matters. A lot. Here's how the typical cleaning time breaks down by house size:

- Small (under 1,000 sq ft): 1.5 to 2.5 hours
- Medium (1,000-2,000 sq ft): 2.5 to 4 hours
- Large (2,000-3,000 sq ft): 4 to 6 hours
- Extra Large (over 3,000 sq ft): 5 to 8 hours

That's just the time. Multiply by the hourly rate for deep cleaning ($75-$110) and you'll see why a large house can cost $400-$600 easily. The calculator handles this automatically based on your inputs.

sections.heading: Frequency Discounts: Save Money with Regular Deep Cleaning
sections.body:
Here's a pro tip: schedule deep cleaning regularly and you'll pay less per visit. Most services offer discounts for recurring bookings:

- Weekly: 15-30% off (typical 20%)
- Biweekly: 10-20% off (typical 15%)
- Monthly: 5-10% off (typical 7%)

In my experience, biweekly deep cleaning is the sweet spot. You keep the house in great shape without the sticker shock of a full deep clean every time. The calculator includes these discounts — just select your frequency.

sections.heading: Deep Cleaning vs Standard Cleaning: Which Should You Choose?
sections.body:
Not sure which service you need? Here's the short version:

Choose deep cleaning if:
- You're moving in or out of a home
- It's been more than 3 months since a thorough clean
- You have pets or kids (the grime builds up fast)
- You're preparing for a special event or guests

Choose standard cleaning if:
- You clean regularly and just need maintenance
- Your home is generally tidy
- You're on a tighter budget

Honestly, this won't work if your walls are textured — some deep cleaning services charge extra for textured walls because they trap dust. Check with your provider before booking.

  • Provide your own supplies: Some cleaners charge a surcharge for eco-friendly or premium products. If you have a preferred brand, ask if you can supply it—this can save $10–$20 per visit.
